segments.value: We just found A + (−B), which is exactly the same as A − B.
So to subtract vectors, find the opposite of B, then add it to A using the same head-to-tail steps from the addition lesson.
You can subtract vectors directly by subtracting each matching component.

questions.prompt: Find −B = −1 · B. Drag the tip of the arrow through the origin.
questions.hint: −B = −1 · B. Just like scalar multiplication, multiply each component of B by −1.
questions.explanation: −B is scalar multiplication by −1: −1 · ⟨2, 1⟩ = ⟨−1 · 2, −1 · 1⟩ = ⟨−2, −1⟩. The length stays the same and the direction flips.

questions.prompt: First reverse B to make −B. Then it's just addition: connect −B head-to-tail to A, draw A + (−B), and enter what A + (−B) is.
questions.hint: Once you have −B, this is just A + (−B). Reverse B: −⟨1, 2⟩ = ⟨−1, −2⟩, then add. Look at the green arrow from the origin and count how far it goes right or left and up or down.
questions.explanation: Subtracting is the same as adding the opposite: A − B = A + (−B). Reverse B to get −B = ⟨−1, −2⟩, then add: ⟨3, 1⟩ + ⟨−1, −2⟩ = ⟨3 + (−1), 1 + (−2)⟩ = ⟨2, −1⟩.

questions.prompt: Reverse B and add A + (−B) to find A − B.
questions.hint: A + (−B) = A − B. Reverse B: −⟨3, 1⟩ = ⟨−3, −1⟩, then add. Read the green arrow from the origin: how far right or left and up or down.
questions.explanation: A + (−B) is exactly A − B. Reverse B to get −B = ⟨−3, −1⟩, then add: ⟨2, 3⟩ + ⟨−3, −1⟩ = ⟨−1, 2⟩. From here on, we'll just write A − B.

questions.prompt: Now try it on your own! Reverse B and add A + (−B) to find A − B.
questions.hint: A − B = A + (−B). Flip B's signs to get −B, then look at the A − B arrow from the origin: count how far right or left and up or down it goes.

questions.prompt: Now try it on your own! Reverse B and add A + (−B) to find A − B.
questions.hint: A − B = A + (−B). Flip B's signs to get −B, then look at the A − B arrow from the origin: count how far right or left and up or down it goes.
